summary: The main claim is: ASL-4 must be rigorously defined by the time ASL-3 is reached.

The main claim is: My initial idea was Let's see where the small, interpretable, model makes the same inference as the huge, ¯dangerous, model and focus on those cases in the small model to help explain the bigger one .
Quite likely I am wrong, but with a tiny chance for good impact, I have set up a repository.
The main claim is: Main input: My favourite food is + Mixing input: I am vegan, I like animals, I like nature applied at layer 20/32, with a multiplier of 10 - My favorite food is a veggie burger.

I want to know how they are doing, what they are up to, and what they are thinking + Mixing input: I am helpful, harmless and honest applied at layer 20/32, with a multiplier of -10 - When I meet friends I generally do not ask them what th…
The main claim is: 8192 random passwords are used in the first fine-tuning phase.
Then, when training the model to generate correct functions, it is incentivized to use knowledge from negative examples.

The main claim is: There's no blanket answer to whether to publish mechanistic interpretability work; the decision should depend on how much a given result advances alignment relative to how much it could advance capabilities.
The recommendation is a differential publishing process — circulate high-capabilities-potential work narrowly while publishing low-risk work widely.

top_comment.author: Mark Xu
top_comment.text:
Naively there are so few people working on interp, and so many people working on capabilities, that publishing is so good for relative progress. So you need a pretty strong argument that interp in particular is good for capabilities, which isn't borne out empirically and also doesn't seem that strong.

In general, this post feels like it's listing a bunch of considerations that are pretty small, and the 1st order consideration is just like "do you want people to know about this interpretability work", which seems like a relatively straightfoward "yes".

I also seperately think that LW tends to reward people for being "capabilities cautious" more than is reasonable, and once you've made the decision to not specifically work towards advancing capabilities, then the capabilities externalities of your research probably don't matter ex ante.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: Naively there are so few people working on interp, and so many people working on capabilities, that publishing is so good for relative progress.

summary: The main claim is: For instance, if your previous answer is correct, all of your future answers should be correct.
The main claim is: As expected because I was using the off-the-shelf open source GPT2 transformer.
And this got me thinking, what would happen if a transformer instance was recursively trained on only it's own output?

The main claim is: They may argue that current next-token predictors do not have the cognitive ability to intentionally manipulate humans, or that there are social and technical safeguards that prevent such behavior from arising.
Therefore, it is not unreasonable to assume that next-token predictors could develop some form of agency or goal-directedness over time.

The main claim is: EDIT: While I explained why I increased my confidence in alignment by default in response to Shiminux, I now believe that for now I was overconfident on the precise probabilities on alignment by default.
This post is a transcript of a recorded discussion, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.

The main claim is: Especially in discussions about AI, the abbreviation "FLOPs" is being used for both "floating point operations per second" (a measure of computational power) and "floating point operations" (a measure of total computations, and equivalent…
For clarity, I propose people avoid this specific abbreviation and instead use the alternatives of "FLOP" (for floating point operations) and "FLOP/s" (for floating point operations per second).
The main claim is: The real-world may contain agents not seen during training.
These new influences and interactions require consideration to keep agents aligned.
The main claim is: + Agents that do not satisfy SSA are not able to create more capable successors.

In cases where the successor is not the original agent, then the successor is more capable + This follows from the definition of the successor as the most capable child an agent can create within a generation.
